title: Modal Verbs
subtitle: Ability, possibility, obligation, deduction — present and past
intro: Modal verbs encode the writer's stance. 'Governments should invest' is a recommendation; 'Governments must invest' is an obligation; 'Governments may invest' is a possibility. Using the wrong modal sends the wrong message. In Task 2, modal precision is how you sound persuasive without overreaching — and 'must have' vs. 'may have' vs. 'should have' for past deduction is a hallmark of Band 7+ control.

branches.label: Deduction
children: must be = I'm sure it is
can't be = I'm sure it isn't

rules.heading: Present modals by function
rules.body: Ability: can, be able to. Possibility: may, might, could. Obligation: must, have to, should, ought to. Deduction: must (certain), can’t (impossible), might/could/may (possible).

rules.heading: Past modals: modal + have + V3
rules.body: 'must have done' = logical certainty about the past; 'might/may/could have done' = past possibility; 'should have done' = past advice / regret; 'can’t have done' = past impossibility.

rules.heading: Must vs. have to
rules.body: 'Must' = internal/author-driven obligation ('Applicants must submit…'); 'have to' = external rule ('I have to submit it by Friday'). 'Mustn’t' = prohibition; 'don’t have to' = no obligation.

rules.heading: Should vs. ought to vs. had better
rules.body: 'Should' is the everyday advice word; 'ought to' is slightly more formal and moral; 'had better' carries urgency or warning ('You had better leave now').

examples.sentence: Governments must prioritise renewable energy if climate targets are to be met.
examples.note: 'Must' for strong obligation in author's voice.

examples.sentence: Unregulated algorithms could entrench existing biases if left unchecked.
Policymakers should have anticipated the social cost of automation.
That claim can’t be true — the data clearly contradict it.
Children who are read to regularly may develop stronger verbal skills.

band6.text: The government must to solve this problem because poor people can not survive.
band6.issue: 'must to' is incorrect, and blunt 'must' sounds commanding rather than analytical.

band8.text: The government ought to intervene, as low-income households may otherwise struggle to meet basic needs.
band8.why: 'ought to' softens the obligation to expert-advisory register, 'may otherwise struggle' hedges prediction appropriately.

band8_marker: Band 8 writers stack modals with adverbs for finely-calibrated certainty: 'might conceivably', 'could plausibly', 'may well', 'must undoubtedly'. They also use modal passives to sound authoritative without being strident: 'It could be argued that…', 'The findings should be interpreted with caution.' This modal hedging is exactly the register examiners associate with academic maturity.

common_errors.fix: Modals take bare infinitive → 'You must arrive on time.'
For negative deduction, use 'can’t' → 'He can’t be the thief.'

common_errors.error: Using 'may' in questions ('May it rain tomorrow?')
common_errors.fix: Use 'might' or 'is it likely' for questions: 'Might it rain tomorrow?'

practice.title: Rewrite using the modal in brackets
practice.instruction: Change the sentence using the target modal, keeping the meaning.

items.prompt: It is necessary that all applicants submit proof of identity. (must)
items.answer: All applicants must submit proof of identity.

items.prompt: Perhaps he forgot the meeting. (might have)
items.answer: He might have forgotten the meeting.

items.prompt: It was a mistake not to invest in vaccination earlier. (should have)
items.answer: Governments / we should have invested in vaccination earlier.

items.prompt: I am certain she is lying. (must)
It is possible that the data were manipulated. (may)
items.answer: The data may have been manipulated.

items.prompt: It is impossible that he remembered the password. (can’t have)
items.answer: He can’t have remembered the password.

items.prompt: I recommend that you rewrite the introduction. (should)
It is not necessary to attend every session. (don’t have to)
items.answer: You don’t have to attend every session.

practice.title: Pick the modal
practice.instruction: Type the correct modal or modal + have + V3.

items.prompt: The report is dated 2019, so the data ___ (be) more recent than that.
She ___ (leave) already — her bag is gone.
Students ___ (submit) their essays by Friday or they will lose marks.
The minister denies any wrongdoing but ___ (know) about the scheme.
items.answer: may have known / might have known

items.prompt: You ___ (not tell) him the whole story; it would only have worried him.
Even experienced drivers ___ (make) mistakes in heavy rain.
Visitors ___ (not smoke) anywhere on the premises.
I ___ (not find) my keys anywhere; I ___ (leave) them at the office.

practice.title: Modal nuance
practice.instruction: Pick the modal that best fits the context.

items.prompt: The government ___ take action on housing; the crisis is deepening.
He is nowhere to be seen. He ___ gone home early.
Children ___ exposed to multiple languages acquire them more easily.
The bridge ___ collapsed because of a structural flaw, but investigators have not ruled out sabotage.
Students ___ prepare thoroughly; otherwise they risk low scores.
Passengers ___ use mobile phones during take-off.
The findings ___ to generalise to other populations.
That can’t be John — he ___ in Tokyo.
